content.text:
A **spanning tree** connects all vertices with 
minimal edges.

content.title: Spanning Tree
content.content:
A **spanning tree** of connected graph $G = (V, E)$ is a subgraph 
$T = (V, E')$ that:

- Includes all vertices of $G$

- Is a tree (connected and acyclic)

content.title: Existence of Spanning Trees
content.content: Every connected graph has a spanning tree.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Start with $G$. While the graph has a cycle, remove one edge from the 
cycle (keeping the graph connected). When no cycles remain, we have a 
spanning tree.

content.text:
In a **weighted graph**, each edge has a 
numerical weight (cost, distance, etc.).

content.title: Minimum Spanning Tree
content.content:
A **minimum spanning tree** (MST) of a 
weighted connected graph is a spanning tree with minimum total edge weight.

content.title: Network design
content.problem:
To connect $n$ cities with roads of varying construction costs, an MST 
gives the cheapest network connecting all cities.

content.title: Prim's Algorithm
content.content:
1. Start with any vertex.

2. Repeatedly add the minimum-weight edge connecting a tree vertex 
 to a non-tree vertex.

3. Stop when all vertices are in the tree.

content.title: Kruskal's Algorithm
content.content:
1. Sort all edges by weight.

2. For each edge in order: add it to the tree if it doesn't create 
 a cycle.

3. Stop when $n - 1$ edges are added.

content.title: Finding MST
content.problem:
Graph with vertices $A, B, C, D$ and edges: $AB(1), BC(4), CD(2), AD(3), 
BD(5), AC(6)$.

Kruskal: Sort edges: $AB(1), CD(2), AD(3), BC(4), BD(5), AC(6)$.

Add $AB(1)$, $CD(2)$, $AD(3)$. Now $BC$ would create cycle. Done!

MST weight: $1 + 2 + 3 = 6$.

content.title: Correctness of Greedy MST Algorithms
content.content: Both Prim's and Kruskal's algorithms produce a minimum spanning tree.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The proof uses the "cut property": the minimum-weight edge crossing any 
cut is in some MST.

marginNotes.text:
Unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground 
and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it produces 
many seeds.
